text: First, it was power banks. Now, it's
everything. After multiple in-flight
fire scares and emergency landings
caused by overheated batteries, airlines
have gone into full clampdown mode.
Carryons in 2025 aren't just about size
anymore. They're about what's inside,
how it's packed, and whether it ticks
even one red flag. If your bag doesn't
follow the latest rules on batteries,
bags, or banned items, it won't make it
past the gate. and maybe neither will
you. Here's how to avoid the carry-on
chaos before it
starts. If you fly often, there's
probably a power bank in your bag right
now. It keeps your phone alive, your
headphones charged, and your sanity
intact. But here's what most travelers
don't realize. Power banks come with
rules, and messing up could get you
pulled aside at security or worse,
pulled off the plane. This isn't just
airport paranoia. In January 2025, a
power bank caught fire on an Air Busan
flight before takeoff. It triggered a
full evacuation. The kicker, that
battery was actually allowed on board.
Legal size, still caused panic. So,
let's get clear. Anything over 160 W
hours, around 32,000 MIA, is completely
banned. Doesn't matter if it's in your
carry-on or checked luggage. It's a
no-go. And no, draining the battery to
zero doesn't help. What matters is the
capacity it was built with, not how much
charge is left. Even if it's empty, the
rules still apply. Under 100 W hours,
that's the sweet spot. You can bring it,
but only in your carry-on. Checked
baggage? Absolutely not. If your power
bank is between 100 and 160 W hours,
you'll need airline approval, and even
then, you're only allowed two. That's
it. Now, here's why checked bags are off
limits. If a battery overheats down in
the cargo hold, no one's there to stop a
fire. And fires on planes, yeah, they
don't end well. What's new in 2025? Some
airlines are stepping things up.
Passengers are being asked to declare
every single power bank at check-in,
even the tiny ones. People on Reddit
have been sharing stories about being
told they could only carry two,
regardless of size, because that's what
the airlines said that day. So before
you head to the airport, check your
batteries rating, pack it in your
carry-on, and read your airlines policy.
The rules aren't always clear, but the
consequences
are. Got one of those sleek, techy
suitcases with a built-in battery?
Better read the fine print because in
2025, airlines aren't playing nice with
smart luggage that doesn't play by the
rules. If the battery can't be removed,
your bag might not even make it past
check-in. Sounds harsh, but that's the
reality. Airports don't care how
futuristic your suitcase looks. If the
power source is locked in place, it's a
no-fly zone. And unless you enjoy
lastminute shopping for overpriced
luggage at the terminal, you'll want to
make sure your battery pops out easily.
But here's where it gets messy. Even if
the battery is removable, that doesn't
guarantee you're in the clear. Travelers
on Reddit are already complaining about
smart bags getting rejected. Why?
Because some airlines are now asking for
proof, like manufacturer certification
or compliance with newer safety
standards like UL 2054 for lithium
batteries. Most people don't even know
what that is, let alone where to find
the paperwork. The fix? Before your next
trip, dig up the manual, check the
label, or reach out to the manufacturer.
Better yet, contact your airline. If
they're vague or inconsistent, which
happens more than it should, consider
switching to a model with a clearly
detachable battery and visible safety
marking. It's 2025. Your bag can charge
your phone, but if it can't pass airport
security, it's not that
smart. Thought the liquid rule finally
died? Not quite. A few UK airports made
headlines for scrapping the classic
100mill limit, letting people breeze
through security with full-size bottles
and zero plastic bag origami. It sounded
like progress until reality showed up.
Turns out only a handful of airports
actually made the switch and some of
them didn't even stick with it. By mid
2024, six airports, including London
City, Aberdine, and Newcastle, brought
the old rule roaring back, thanks to,
you guessed it, security concerns. So,
if you're flying out of the UK, don't
assume anything. One terminal might wave
you through with a liter of shampoo. The
next might force you to toss it, or
worse, chug it in front of everyone.
Now, let's talk about the EU. The plan
was bold. Roll out advanced CT scanners
across Europe. kill off the 100 millm
rule by the end of 2024 and make the
security line feel less like a
punishment. But here we are in 2025 and
the reality, most airports didn't
upgrade in time. The ones that did, many
still can't use the scanners for
international flights. So yes, you're
still cramming toothpaste into
travelsized tubes across the continent.
And it gets trickier. Heathro, for
example, has installed the fancy
scanners, but only in certain terminals
and not for every destination. Some
international flights are still stuck in
the past with the full 100 mm crackdown.
Worse yet, if you're connecting through
an airport that doesn't have the same
scanner system, guess what happens?
You'll likely have to ditch every single
liquid you just got cleared for at your
departure gate. Doesn't matter that you
pass security once. Your layover airport
can and will start you from scratch.
Bottom line, the 100 millimeter rule
isn't dead. It's just confused. Until
every airport agrees on the rules and
has the tech to match, your best bet is
to play it old school. Stick to the
clear plastic bag. Keep everything under
100 milll and never trust a headline to
pack your carry-on for
you. Flying with a lowcost carrier in
Europe? Don't assume your carry-on's
safe. Ryionaire, EasyJet, Whiz Air,
they've all quietly shrunk the size
limits for free bags. That backpack you
used last summer might cost you this
time. And they're strict. If your bag
doesn't fit inside their little metal
frame at the gate, expect to pay.
Doesn't matter if it almost fits or if
you measured it at home. If it doesn't
slide in perfectly, they'll charge you
on the spot. So, now it's a choice.
shrink your luggage or open your wallet.
Many travelers are giving in and buying
smaller airline approved bags. Others
pay the extra fee to check their
carry-on or upgrade to priority boarding
just to bring a normal size bag on
board, often without realizing that
upgrade isn't cheap either. Reddit's
full of stories. People being charged
even when their bags technically met the
size, just not gate friendly enough.
Some seasoned flyers now carry measuring
tape with them. Yes, really. Here's the
play. Before you fly, check your
airlines baggage policy. Don't rely on
memory or old info. If you're flying
multiple budget airlines, compare them
side by side. And if you're not ready to
downsize, prepay for the bigger
allowance. It's cheaper than being
ambushed at the gate. In 2025, flying
cheap doesn't mean packing easy. The
overhead bin space is shrinking, but the
fees, not so much.
Flying with Air Canada on a basic
economy ticket in 2025, you might want
to read the fine print because that
basic is about to feel real basic.
Starting January 3rd, forget about
rolling your carry-on on board for free.
You're allowed one personal item only. A
small backpack, a laptop bag, maybe a
purse. That little suitcase, it's going
to cost you. First carryon,
$35. Add another, that'll be $65 per
piece. Yep, even that tiny hard shell
that used to fly free now comes with a
price tag. But wait, there's more.
Beginning January 21st, basic economy
passengers won't get to choose their
seats for free anymore. Instead, you'll
be randomly assigned hello, middle seat,
unless you cough up extra cash to pick
your spot. You used to be able to choose
during online check-in at no charge. Not
anymore. And this isn't just Air Canada
tightening the belt. United, Delta, and
a few others are playing the same game,
charging for carryons and turning seat
selection into a paid perk. Check
Reddit, and you'll find plenty of
passengers saying they paid more, only
to get stuck in a middle seat anyway.
One traveler even shared that despite
booking with their partner under the
same reservation, they were assigned
separate seats in both directions.
Here's what savvy travelers are doing.
Checking in early. Some folks say that
logging in the minute check-in opens
gives you better chances at an aisle or
window, even if it's not guaranteed.
Worth a shot if you'd rather not get
sandwiched. So, if you're flying basic
economy, budget for more than just the
ticket, because the cheap option, it's
stacking up fees faster than your
boarding group gets
called. Sure, we all know the basics. No
knives, no fireworks, no gallons of
shampoo stuffed in your backpack. But
the list of banned items goes deeper
than you think. Some things are obvious,
others not so much. And getting stopped
at security because of a random item in
your bag, it's not a fun way to start
your trip. Let's start with the
classics. Anything sharp. Knives,
scissors, multi-tools, even giant nail
clippers. If it cuts, pokes, or stabs,
it's not flying with you. That includes
things like throwing stars. Yes, someone
actually tried. Hatchets, drills, and
tools over 10 cm. Got gear? Check it in.
No exceptions. Next up, weapons. Real or
fake. Guns, ammo, stun guns, tasers, or
even that super realistic movie prop you
thought was a cool conversation starter.
Leave it at home unless you enjoy long
chats with TSA and zero chance of making
your gate. Firearms only with paperwork.
Lots of it. Then there's the flammable
stuff. Fireworks, gas canisters,
cleaning sprays, and even some aerosols
are a flatout no. Doesn't matter if it's
your carry-on or checked bag, flammables
get grounded every time. But here's
where it gets weird. Some travelers have
been stopped over flour or turmeric
powder or bags of coffee grounds.
Certain countries flag powder-like
substances as potential security
threats. And if you're carrying anything
that looks like it could be part of a
science project, be ready for questions.
Flying with an old camcorder or vintage
electronics. Some travelers have been
pulled aside because airport staff
couldn't figure out what kind of battery
was inside or whether it was the type
that might randomly explode mid-flight.
Here's the pro move. Check TSA's
prohibited items list before you pack.
And if you're flying internationally,
double check the rules at your
destination, too. What's allowed in the
US might get flagged elsewhere. And if
you're traveling with meds, baby
formula, or anything unusual? Bring the
paperwork. No one wants to explain
powdered protein to a customs officer
without proof. Think you've covered the
obvious? Let's talk about the oddly
specific stuff. Things you'd never
expect to be banned, but absolutely are.
Got a thermometer in your bag? Better
check what kind. Because if it contains
mercury, it's not flying with you. Not
even close. You might think mercury
thermometers are extinct, replaced by
digital gadgets and forehead scanners.
But they're still around. In some parts
of the world, they're cheap, easy to
find, and surprisingly still in use.
Here's the problem. Mercury eats
aluminum. And since aircraft are
basically giant flying aluminum tubes,
that's a nightmare waiting to happen. If
the mercury leaks, it can break down the
protective oxide layer on the metal,
making it brittle and structurally weak.
That's not just a chemistry issue, it's
an aviation hazard. So yeah, that tiny
glass tube, total flight risk. Go
digital or go
home. Going off the grid, great. Just
don't expect your tent poles to come
with you in the cabin. TSA sees them as
long metal objects with too much
potential for trouble. If it's rigid,
pointy, or sounds like something you'd
use to roast marshmallows or build a
shelter, check it in. Otherwise, expect
delays and some raised eyebrows at
security. That cute little multi-tool on
your keychain still might not make the
cut, literally. Scissors are allowed,
but only if the blades are shorter than
4 in. Even then, it depends on the
agent's vibe that day. Box cutters,
large shears, and anything even thinking
about being a knife, nope. Rule of
thumb, if it has a blade, check it.
Unless you enjoy surrendering it with a
shrug at the
checkpoint. It's plastic. It's neon.
It's clearly for fun. Doesn't matter.
Security doesn't play real or fake at 6
a.m. with a line of passengers behind
you. If it even vaguely resembles a
weapon, foam, toy, cosplay, prop,
whatever, it's getting flagged. So,
unless you're cool with an awkward
confiscation in front of your kid, just
leave it
behind. You're flying to a game? Cool.
But your gear isn't flying in the cabin.
Baseball bats, golf clubs, hockey
sticks, lacrosse sticks, anything long
and swingable stays in the hold. TSA
doesn't care if you're the next MVP. If
it can whack, it gets packed.
It's just flour or protein powder or
bath salts. Totally harmless, right?
Well, TSA doesn't see it that way.
Powders over 12 oz, 350 migolds, get
flagged because they can hide
explosives. Baby formula, meds, and
cremated remains usually get a pass, but
expect extra screening. So, yeah, your
artisal baking project might trigger a
full bag search.
Bringing your toaster, your drill, your
wrench the size of your forearm. Think
again. Big tools and bulky appliances
are automatic nos in the cabin. If it
needs a plug or weighs like a dumbbell,
it belongs in checked baggage. And if
you're trying to carry on a power drill,
maybe rethink some life
choices. Let's talk about surviving the
security checkpoint, especially when
your bag is basically a mini electronic
store. If you're traveling for business
or just can't live without your gear,
your carry-on probably includes a phone
or three, a laptop, a tablet, a camera,
power banks, chargers, maybe even a
drone. All the fun stuff, and
unfortunately, all the stuff security
loves to flag and thieves love to spot.
Here's how to get through without stress
or losing half your tech. Pack smart.
Put all electronics and valuables in the
same compartment or pouch, not scattered
all over your bag. You'll need to pull
them out anyway, so don't make yourself
play hide-and-seek with TSA. At the
scanner, step aside on purpose. Don't
fumble in the middle of the line. Move
to a clear space, grab a tray, and take
your time. Lay out your electronics in
one single tray. No layering, no
stacking. Security needs to see each
item clearly, and you'll avoid extra
screening. Never let that tray out of
your sight. Stand next to it, hand on
the edge if you must. Most thefts at
airports happen right here. Seconds of
distraction and poof, that MacBook's
gone. Don't walk through the scanner
until your tray is about to go in, too.
And most importantly, slow down. No
flight is taking off without you if
you've already made it to the
checkpoint. Rushing is how people leave
phones in bins, forget chargers, or
trigger alarms by missing a battery pack
buried under a scarf. Control your pace.
Control the outcome. Moving fast doesn't
make you efficient. Being methodical
does. Travel in 2022 isn't just about
packing smart. It's about packing a
wear. Your carry-on isn't just being
judged for weight and dimensions
anymore. Every charger, every zipper
pocket, every bottle, every battery,
it's all fair game. Rules are changing
faster than airport terminals can keep
up, and not knowing them can cost you
time, money, or worse, a missed flight.
So, don't guess, don't assume, and
definitely don't rely on what worked
last year. Check the rules, pack with
intention, and treat your carry-on like
a VIP guest, because in 2025, getting to
your gate is only half the journey.
Getting through it is where the real
game begins.
